english: auspices Glr.; sai (sa'i) dpyad (dpyad) bzań-bar (bzang bar) śés-pa (shes pa) to know that this examination will turn out favourably Glr.; *rin ċád - če* (rin cad che) (gen. written *bcad-če* (bcad ce), cf. bcód-pa (bcod pa) extr.) W. to tax, to estimate; goṅ-táṅ (gong thang) dpyád-kyis (dpyad kyis) (or -pas (-pas)) mi (mi) čod (chod) Glr. v. čod-pa (chod pa) 2; esp. in medicine: smán-pas (sman pas) ... dpyad (dpyad) byás-te (byas te) ... żes (zhes) dpyad (dpyad) byás-so (byas so) the physician having tried, tried thus, (pronounced the following as the result of his examination) Dzl. N 2, 12; sman-dpyád (sman dpyad) byéd-pa (byed pa) to treat medically, dpyad (dpyad) má-la (ma la) bya (bya) then the mother (not the child) must be placed under medical treatment Lt.; brás-kyis ('bras kyis) btsún-moi (btsun mo'i) sman-dpyád (sman dpyad) byed-pa (byed pa) to cure (the illness of) the queen with rice Dzl.; sman-dpyád-la (sman dpyad la) mkás-pa (mkhas pa) to be skilled in medical science Dzl.; ča-byád (cha byad) dpyád-kyi (dpyad kyi) gnas (gnas) instrumental therapeutics i.e. surgery S.g.; rtog-dpyód (rtog dpyod), brtag-dpyád (brtag dpyad), examination; rtog-dpyód (rtog dpyod) ráb-tu (rab tu) gtóṅ-ba (gtong ba) to examine very closely Pth.; rtog-dpyód (rtog dpyod) toṅ (thong) examine! Mil.; bzaṅ-dpyód (bzang dpyod) examining the worth of a thing. — dpyód-pa-pa (dpyod pa pa), and spyód-pa-pa (spyod pa pa), Ssk. मीमांसक, an Indian sect of philosophers (the former of the two spellings seems to be more correct).

(resp. ydaṅs (ydangs) Cs.), *ṭál-wa* (tal wa), Ld. *śrál-wa* (shral wa), forehead, dprál-bai (dpral ba'i) mda (mda') an arrow sticking in the forehead Glr.; dprál-bai (dpral ba'i) mig (mig) bżin-du (bzhin du) 'like the eye of the countenance', to designate something highly valued (as the scriptural 'apple of the eye'); dprál-bai (dpral ba'i) ṗyógs-kyi (phyogs kyi) ṭad (thad) dráṅ-na (drang na) just before one in front Wdn.; fig. *ṭal-wa (tal wa) ṅán-pa* (ngan pa) W. unlucky; a luckless person.
(or prul-prúl (prul prul)?), *ṭul-ṭúl-la (tul tul la) tóṅ (tong) wa* C. to hang one's self.
as second part of compounds inst. of págs-pa (pags pa), e.g. wá-lpags (wa lpags) fox-skin, stág-lpags (stag lpags) tiger-skin; śún-lpags (shun lpags) skin, bark, peel, shell.

1. v. dpa (dpa). — 2. also sba (sba), cane (seems to be distinguished from smyúg-ma (smyug ma) more in a popular and practical way, than scientifically); spa-skór (spa skor) hoop of a cask Schr.; spa-kár (spa dkar) Mil., spa-lcág (spa lcag) Mil., spa-bér (spa ber) Pth., spa-dbyúg (spa dbyug) Lex., walking-cane; spa-glíṅ (spa gling) cane-flute Sch.; spa-tíl (spa til) lunt, match, v. ṗa-tíl (pha til); spa-dóṅ (spa dong) or -ldoṅ (-ldong) little cask, made of bamboo prob. = gur-gúr (gur gur) dóṅ-mo (dong mo); *pa-bár* (pa bar) C., W. torch; spa-dmyúg (spa dmyug) or -smyug (-smyug), cane Cs.; pa-śíṅ (pa shing) Sik. strong bamboo sticks.
1. juniper, Juniperus squamosa, and some other small species; cf. śúg-pa (shug pa). — 2. cypress Sik.
1. v. spóg-pa (spog pa). — 2. *kál-pag (kal pag) pág-cė* (pag ce) W. to smack (in eating). — 3. C., W. to dip, e.g. meat into the gravy; cf. the following.

resp. skyu-rúm (skyu rum), 1. C. = zan (zan) (= *kó-lag* (ko lag), *pág-ku* (pag ku) Ld.), pap, esp. made of tea and 'tsampa'. — 2. W. = *śa-rúg* (sha rug) C.), sauce, gravy, for dipping in (sops); *dam-pág* (dam pag) W. mire, sludge. — 3. food, dish, mess; W., C. *pag na so-só* (pag na so so).

I. also spaṅ-po (spang po), 1. turf, green-sward, meadow, mdún-na (mdun na) spaṅ-po (spang po) mé-tog (me tog) bkra (bkra) in front a flowery meadow-ground Mil. — 2. moss, also ču-spaṅ (chu spang) Cs. — 3. bog, spaṅ-skóṅ (spang skong) 1. p.n. ('turf-ditch'), a large valley, with a lake in it, on the frontier of Ladak and Rudog. 2. spaṅ-skóṅ (spang skong) ṗyag-rgyá-pa (phyag rgya pa) n. of an ancient work on religion Glr.; spaṅ-rgyan (spang rgyan) a medicinal herb Med.; spaṅ-čan (spang can) covered with turf; spaṅ-ču (spang chu) green mud Sch.; spaṅ-ljóṅs (spang ljongs) grassy country; spaṅ-táṅ (spang thang) a plain covered with verdure; spaṅ-spós (spang spos) Wald-heimia tridactylites, a pretty, very aromatic composite, growing on the higher alps; spaṅ-bóg (spang bog) piece of turf, sod; spaṅ-ma (spang ma) Med., Ssk. तुत्थ, blue vitriol; spaṅ-rtsí (spang rtsi) S.g. (?); spaṅ-żún (spang zhun) verdigris Sch.; spaṅ-rí (spang ri) a grassy hill Mil.; spaṅ-gżóṅ (spang gzhong) a mountain-meadow Mil. II. board, plank, gen. spaṅ-léb (spang leb) Glr. and vulg.; also a slab, slate, flag Lh.; spaṅ-sgó (spang sgo) board or panel of a door Cs.; spaṅ-krí (spang khri) Schr., *ṭi-páṅ* (ti pang) Ld., *paṅ-dáṅ* (pang dang) Ld., book-stand.
only in ṗa-spád (pha spad) father and children; cf. the more frq. ma-smád (ma smad), Lex.
